title: Symplectic fillings and star surgery
speaker: Laura Starkston
abstract: Although the existence of a symplectic filling is well-understood for many contact 3-manifolds, complete classifications of all symplectic fillings of a particular contact manifold are more rare. Relying on a recognition theorem of McDuff for closed symplectic manifolds, we can understand this classification for certain Seifert fibered spaces with their canonical contact structures. In fact, even without complete classification statements, the techniques used can suggest constructions of symplectic fillings with interesting topology.

proof_stages.label: Seifert-plumbing filling problem
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes negative-definite star-shaped plumbings whose boundaries are Seifert fibered spaces over S^2 with the canonical contact structure, and formulates the problem of classifying their minimal symplectic fillings.

proof_stages.label: Finiteness and algorithmic classification theorem
proof_stages.summary: Under the central-weight restriction, minimal fillings have only finitely many possible Euler characteristics and signatures, and under stronger smallness or negativity hypotheses the combinatorial algorithm gives finitely many diffeomorphism types with explicit handlebody or Lefschetz-fibration descriptions.

proof_stages.label: Concave cap and McDuff reduction
proof_stages.summary: The boundary data determines a concave plumbing cap which glues to any filling to form a closed symplectic 4-manifold containing a positive sphere, so McDuff's theorem identifies the closed manifold as a blowup of CP^2 and reduces the filling problem to classifying embeddings of the cap.

proof_stages.label: Homological embeddings via holomorphic blowdowns
proof_stages.summary: By choosing a tame almost-complex structure making the cap spheres holomorphic and blowing down holomorphic exceptional spheres, the embedding is converted into a configuration of projective lines whose possible blowup data are encoded by a finite homological and intersection-combinatorial problem.

proof_stages.label: Isotopy of line configurations and complements
proof_stages.summary: To turn the homological classification into diffeomorphism types, line configurations are classified up to isotopy preserving multi-intersection points, using Gromov's uniqueness of J-holomorphic lines under the stronger hypotheses; cutting out the resulting embedded cap gives the filling.

proof_stages.label: Realizing fillings and defining star surgery
proof_stages.summary: The allowed homological embeddings can often be realized by complex line configurations and blowups, producing explicit non-plumbing fillings; replacing a star-shaped plumbing neighborhood by such another filling is the operation called star surgery.

proof_stages.label: Exotic four-manifold applications and rational-blowdown comparison
proof_stages.summary: Joint work applies star surgery to configurations in blowups of elliptic surfaces, computes fundamental groups, Euler characteristics, signatures, and Seiberg-Witten invariants to obtain exotic CP^2 blowups, and shows at least one star surgery is not obtainable by any sequence of rational blowdowns.

examples.name: Many projective lines through one multi-point
examples.note: The hypothetical addition of many lines through the same intersection point illustrates why the isotopy argument is controlled by the number of multi-intersection points rather than simply by the number of lines.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker reasons through configurations of projective lines with multi-intersection points, explaining how blowdowns create triple points, how many lines through one point affect the isotopy problem, and how to isotope them to complex projective lines

passes.reason: A line configuration with many lines through one multi-point collapses many generic double intersections into a maximal-concurrency/minimal-intersection pattern.
